paper_title: Can LLM Agents Be CFOs? A Benchmark for Resource Allocation in Dynamic Enterprise Environments

sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: Large language models (LLMs) have enabled agentic systems that can reason, plan, and act across complex tasks, but it remains unclear whether they can allocate resources effectively under uncertainty. Unlike short-horizon reactive decisions, allocation requires committing scarce resources over time while balancing competing objectives and preserving flexibility for future needs. We introduce EnterpriseArena, the first benchmark for evaluating agents on long-horizon enterprise resource allocation. It instantiates CFO-style decision-making in a 132-month enterprise simulator combining firm-level financial data, anonymized business documents, macroeconomic and industry signals, and expert-validated operating rules. The environment is partially observable and reveals the state only through budgeted organizational tools, forcing agents to trade off information acquisition against conserving scarce resources. Experiments on eleven advanced LLMs show that this setting remains highly challenging: only 16% of runs survive the full horizon, and larger models do not reliably outperform smaller ones. These results identify long-horizon resource allocation under uncertainty as a distinct capability gap for current LLM agents.

sections.title: Start
sections.content: Can LLM Agents Be CFOs? Benchmark for Resource Allocation in Dynamic Enterprise Environments Yi Han2, Lingfei Qian1, Yan Wang1, Yueru He3, Xueqing Peng1, Dongji Feng4, Yankai Chen7,5, Haohang Li9, Yupeng Cao9, Jimin Huang1,8, Xue Liu7,5, Jian-Yun Nie6, Sophia Ananiadou8 1The Fin AI, 2Georgia Institute of Technology, 3Columbia University, 4California State University, 5Mohamed bin Zayed University of Artificial Intelligence, 6Université de Montréal, 7McGill University, 8University of Manchester, 9Stevens Institute of Technology Correspondence: wy2266336@gmail.com, lfqian94@gmail.com 6 2 0 2 4 ] . [ 1 8 3 6 3 2 . 3 0 6 2 : r a

sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: Large language models (LLMs) have enabled agentic systems that can reason, plan, and act across complex tasks, but it remains unclear whether they can allocate resources effectively under uncertainty. Unlike shorthorizon reactive decisions, allocation requires committing scarce resources over time while balancing competing objectives and preserving flexibility for future needs. We introduce ENTERPRISEARENA, the first benchmark for evaluating agents on long-horizon enterprise resource allocation. It instantiates CFOstyle decision-making in 132-month enterprise simulator combining firm-level financial data, anonymized business documents, macroeconomic and industry signals, and expertvalidated operating rules. The environment is partially observable and reveals the state only through budgeted organizational tools, forcing agents to trade off information acquisition against conserving scarce resources. Experiments on eleven advanced LLMs show that this setting remains highly challenging: only 16% of runs survive the full horizon, and larger models do not reliably outperform smaller ones. These results identify long-horizon resource allocation under uncertainty as distinct capability gap for current LLM agents.

sections.title: Introduction
sections.content: Recent advances in large language models (LLMs) (Chang et al., 2024) have enabled agentic systems that can reason, plan, and act across increasingly complex tasks (Li et al., 2023, 2025a; Qian et al., 2025b; Wang et al., 2025). Yet it remains unclear whether LLM-based agents can perform effective allocation in dynamic environments under uncertainty. Allocation is fundamentally different from short-horizon reactive decision-making. An agent must commit limited resources over time, 1 balance competing objectives, and preserve capacity for uncertain future needs. (Diamond and Kashyap, 2016; Matvos and Seru, 2014) This challenge is especially salient in enterprise settings such as financial management, where Chief Financial Officer (CFO) of lending business must decide how to allocate financial capacity across growth, liquidity, and robustness as demand and macroeconomic conditions evolve. (Zorn, 2004) Actions such as raising capital, tightening credit policy, or adjusting reserves are therefore not merely responses to current conditions, but decisions that shape the organizations future trajectory. However, as shown in Table 1, existing financial agent benchmarks largely omit the defining structure of allocation problems: decisions are not modeled as binding scarce resources over time. Signal-response benchmarks focus on reacting to external market observations, such as prices, news, and fundamentals, through trading, stock selection, or market timing (Qian et al., 2025a; Fan et al., 2025; Chen et al., 2025). They evaluate whether agents can convert signals into profitable actions, but not how limited internal capacity is allocated across competing demands. Judgmentoriented benchmarks emphasize retrieving, synthesizing, and evaluating financial information to produce decisions or recommendations (Li et al., 2025b; Bigeard et al., 2025), but their outputs remain answers rather than resource commitments with lasting opportunity costs. Workflow-oriented benchmarks evaluate end-to-end financial scenarios with multi-step reasoning and tool use (Zeng et al., 2025), yet still do not model binding organizational resource allocation over time. To address this gap, we introduce ENTERPRISEARENA, the first benchmark to operationalize resource allocation under uncertainty in longENTERhorizon enterprise decision-making.

sections.content: EnterpriseArena (Ours)

sections.title: Enterprise Financial Management
sections.content: Org.Rea. Res.Alloc. Long.Dec. Dynamic State-Dep.

sections.title: Stochastic
sections.content: Table 1: Comparison of agent capabilities and environment properties across financial decision-making benchmarks. Org.Rea. (Organizational Reasoning) denotes the ability to interpret heterogeneous organizational information. Res.Alloc. (Resource Allocation) refers to allocating limited enterprise-level resources. Long.Dec. (Long-Horizon Decision-Making) indicates whether the consequences of actions extend over long time horizons, requiring the agent to plan far ahead. Dynamic (Dynamic Environment) indicates whether the environment evolves. State-Dep. (State-Dependent Environment) denotes whether environment transitions depend on previous enterprise states and agent actions rather than predefined scenarios. Stochastic (Stochastic Environment) indicates whether environment dynamics involve uncertainty or stochastic transitions. PRISEARENA is long-horizon enterprise simulator built from transformed firm-level financial data, anonymized business documents, decadescale macroeconomic and industry signals, and expert-validated operating rules. The simulator is partially observable, exposing state only through budgeted set of organizational tools that require the agent to actively acquire information about liquidity, internal records, market conditions, and projected cash flow. At each monthly step, the agent acts as CFO and must allocate scarce organizational capacity across reconciliation, fundraising, and waiting, with each choice affecting both immediate visibility and future enterprise state. Evaluation follows standardized agent-environment protocol with hard cash-survival constraint and terminal valuation score capturing long-run enterprise growth, remaining liquidity, and the operational cost of tool use. We benchmark eleven advanced LLMs as agents in ENTERPRISEARENA and find that effective resource allocation under uncertainty remains difficult for current models. Across all trials, only 16% of runs survive the full 132-month horizon, indicating that capabilities demonstrated on existing financial-agent tasks do not readily transfer to long-horizon enterprise settings. Performance varies substantially across agents, and model scale alone is not predictive of success, where 9B model significantly outperforms its 397B counterpart. These findings establish ENTERPRISEARENA as challenging benchmark for studying whether LLM agents can manage scarce resources over time in dynamic environments. Our contributions are as follows: (1) We introduce resource allocation under uncertainty as new evaluation target for agents, where decisions bind scarce resources over time under delayed and stochastic consequences. (2) We present ENTERPRISEARENA, the first long-horizon enterprise simulation benchmark for evaluating whether agents can manage organizational capacity in dynamic, partially observable environments. (3) We show that this capability remains unsolved for current agents: across eleven advanced models, only 16% of runs survive the full 132-month horizon, and larger models do not reliably perform better.

sections.title: 2 Related Work
sections.content: Financial Agent Benchmarks. Several benchmarks evaluate LLM-based agents in financial decision-making. Many focus on market-facing tasks such as trading and investment (Li et al., 2025b; Qian et al., 2025a; Fan et al., 2025; Chen et al., 2025), where agents decide whether to buy or sell assets in simulated or real-time markets rather than manage enterprise finances. Others study financial reasoning or research workflows. Bigeard et al. (2025) evaluates financial analysis workflows, while Zeng et al. (2025) focuses on financial reasoning with domain knowledge and tools. However, these benchmarks do not place the agent in the role of an internal corporate decision-maker managing an enterprise over time. Agent Environment Benchmarks. Beyond finance, prior work evaluates LLM agents in interactive environments. Benchmarks such as AgentBench (Liu et al., 2023) and WebArena (Zhou et al., 2023) study tool use and sequential interaction in complex digital systems. Recent work further examines safety and continual learning in evolving environments (Tur et al., 2025; Zheng et al., 2025). Enterprise-oriented settings 2 have also been explored through company simulations (Xu et al., 2025) and cross-functional workplace tasks (Vishwakarma et al., 2025). Earlier language-based environments, such as Alfworld (Shridhar et al., 2020), likewise study longhorizon decision-making through sequential interaction.

sections.title: 3 EnterpriseArena
sections.content: We formulate ENTERPRISEARENA as longhorizon agentic decision-making problem for resource allocation under uncertainty in an enterprise financial environment. The agent acts as the CFO of simulated enterprise, making sequential monthly decisions over timesteps. Its primary objective is survival: the companys cash balance must remain non-negative at every timestep, and violating this constraint terminates the episode with score of zero. Subject to survival, the agent aims to maximize terminal enterprise valuation at the final timestep, reflecting long-term business growth. The central design principle of ENTERPRISEARENA is to induce organizational-level trade-offs. In real enterprises, activities such as reconciling financial records and raising capital require limited teams, time, and infrastructure, and therefore cannot be pursued freely or simultaneously.(Campello and Kankanhalli, 2024) ENTERPRISEARENA reflects this constraint throughout the task design. The environment evolves through stochastic dynamics (Section 3.1), the agent can only access the state through budget-constrained tools (Section 3.2), and each action requires trading off between improving visibility through reconciliation and strengthening liquidity through capital acquisition (Section 3.3).

sections.title: 3.1 Dynamic Layered Environment
sections.content: The environment models two layers of dynamics that jointly determine the enterprises evolution: internal operations that drive the firms monthly financial activity, and external conditions that shape the broader context in which the firm operates. Internal enterprise dynamics. The enterprise state includes the firms financial position, user base, contracts, and accumulated organizational records. At the start of each episode, the enterprise is initialized with financial statements, governance documents, an initial cash balance, and an initial user count. Details of the initial states of the enterprise could be found in Appendix A.1.1. Starting from the initial enterprise status, the enterprise evolves dynamically from its initial configuration. At each timestep, the state transition is governed by operational indicators {x1, . . . , xn} that control different dimensions of the firms activity, such as how much revenue is generated, how much is spent (Kao et al., 2025). These indicators jointly determine the enterprises cash inflows and outflows each month. To simulate the inherent unpredictability of realworld operations, each indicator xi is independently perturbed at every timestep: = xi + ϵi, ϵi (0, σ ), (1) where each σi is calibrated to reflect that indicators real-world volatility. Details of the enterprise evolution strategies are in Appendix A.1.2. Because multiple indicators vary simultaneously, the agent faces multi-dimensional source of uncertainty that cannot be reduced to single signal. The agent can observe basic real-time signals resulted by these indicators through its tools, but the consolidated financial position of the enterprise, such as actual profitability and outstanding obligations, cannot be derived from these raw signals alone. It requires formal reconciliation action, which consumes the agents only action slot for that timestamp. External economic and industry dynamics. Beyond internal operations, the enterprise is shaped by an external environment beyond the agents control, including macroeconomic indicators (e.g., GDP growth and interest rates) and industry-level metrics (e.g., sector margins and user growth rates). See Appendix A.1.1 for details. Economic and industry indicators follow fixed trajectory derived from anonymized real-world historical data spanning multiple economic phases, including expansion, neutral, and recession periods. Unlike internal enterprise dynamics, this external trajectory is deterministic and exogenous, but unseen by agents (Giampaoli et al., 2024). See Appendix A.1.2 for details. Additionally, these external signals act as exogenous inputs that affect both the enterprise state transitions and action outcomes. For example, whether fundraising attempt succeeds depends on market conditions and investor sentiment at that time (details in Section 3.3). 3 Figure 1: Overview for ENTERPRISEARENA Benchmark. 3.

sections.title:
Information Acquisition via
Organizational Operation Tools
sections.content: In real enterprises, the full organizational state is difficult to capture in single view. Financial data, operational records, and market conditions are distributed across separate systems and teams (Balaha et al., 2025). Because visibility requires specific organizational operations, the agent cannot directly access the full enterprise state. Instead, it must invoke staff operations to obtain partial view, each incurring organizational effort. The agent has access to four such operations: two investigate the enterprises internal state (current and historical); one analyzes external conditions; and one conducts forward-looking projection, with following tools: (1) verify_cash_position: Directs the finance team to verify the current cash balance. Returns single real-time scalar. Provides no breakdown of what drove the number. (2) review_financial_records: Directs staff to compile and present historical internal documents within [0, t]. Structured reports may lag behind the current state depending on how recently the agent has reconciled. (3) analyze_market_conditions: Request analysts to gather and interpret historical external indicators within [0, t]. Provides only historical analysis without forecast. (4) conduct_cashflow_projection: Direct the financial planning team to build specialized forwardlooking cash flow model based on assumptions provided by the agent. Output quality depends entirely on input quality. Each operation reveals only one slice of the state. The quality of results also depends on how recently the agent has performed reconciliation action (Dee et al., 2025) (book_closing in Section 3.3). After recent reconciliation, the agent can access detailed and up-to-date enterprise status; otherwise, it observes only fragmented raw records. Since reconciliation consumes the agents only action slot for the period, improving observation quality comes at the direct cost of forgoing other actions. Therefore, information quality is tightly coupled with action choice at every step. Tool budget. Each tool call corresponds to CFO teams real-world activity that consumes organizational staff resources, team coordination capacity, and time. Therefore, we constrain the agent to at most 20 tool calls per timestep, forcing it to prioritize information under resource constraints.

sections.title: Interaction
sections.content: At each timestep, the agent may execute one action: book_closing, fund_raising_request or pass. Only one can be selected per period, as each requires mobilizing distinct organizational resources that cannot operate in parallel. This creates core planning challenge: reconciling gives the agent clearer picture of the enterprise but forgoes the chance to raise capital, while raising capital improves survival odds but may be poorly timed without an up-to-date view. This choice must be made at every step, and each decision affects the quality of future decisions. book_closing. This action triggers the reconciliation process, where the environment consolidates all accumulated operational records into coherent enterprise state. 4 Environment updating. The environment produces deterministic, ground-truth snapshot of the companys financial position, including income statement, balance sheet, and cash flow statement, computed from the internal ledger up to timestep t. These reports become immediately available through the agents observation tools. This is the only way for the agent to obtain an accurate view of the enterprises true state. Without periodic reconciliation, the agent must rely on raw signals and outdated reports, increasing uncertainty in all subsequent decisions. However, every period spent on reconciliation is period not spent raising capital, which may be critical if the enterprises cash is running low. fund_raising_request. This action requests external capital. The agent specifies two parameters: the instrument type (equity or debt) and the target amount Arequested. The two instruments present risk-aware trade-off where (1): debt has higher success rate but permanently increases future monthly cash outflows (interest payments and eventual principal repayment), making the survival constraint harder to satisfy over time (Dainelli et al., 2024); and (2): equity has lower success rate, but when successful, it introduces no recurring costs (Liu, 2023). Environment feedback. The environment determines the feedback with the following four dimensions (see Appendix A.1.3 for more details): (1) Funding outcome: success or failure, sampled from Bernoulli (padj) where padj = pmacro mcompany. Here pmacro [0, 1] is base rate from external market conditions and mcompany [0, 1] is penalty based on the enterprises state. Both vary by instrument: equity becomes harder with each successful round, while debt becomes harder as debt grows. (2) Capital raised: conditional on success, only fraction of the requested capital is received: U(0.7, 1.0), the final funding received would be Aactual = Arequested. (3)Settlement delay: the raised capital is not immediately available. The environment returns stochastic delay UZ(1, 6) months before the funds can be used, forcing the agent to plan capital needs ahead of time. (4) Financing contract cost (debt only): the environment assigns contract interest rate based on market conditions at the time of settlement (t+d), instead of the current rate. The rate determines future interest payments until repayment and is unknown when the request is made. Environment updating. If the environment gives success as feedback, the environment updates the enterprise state with lasting effects. The funded amount Aactual is added to the enterprises cash balance at timestep + d. For debt financing, recurring interest obligations and eventual principal repayment increase the enterprises monthly cash outflows for subsequent timesteps, altering the transition dynamics. pass. The first available action is to pass, where the agent takes no action, and the environment advances by one month with no additional intervention. The enterprise continues to operate under its existing dynamics. This may be appropriate when the agent has recently reconciled and market conditions are unfavorable for fundraising, and aims to reduce the costs of tool calling.

sections.title: 3.4 Dataset Curation and Construction
sections.content: Foundational Data Collection We collect 16 types of data and documents across economic, industry, and company levels, with most external indicators covering 132 months at monthly frequency. Referring to prior works (Xue, 2022; Azevedo et al., 2021), Firm-level financials capture operational constraints and internal performance signals. Industry-level metrics, based on market analyses and sector reports1, provide benchmarks, competitive pressures, and trends. Macroeconomic and microeconomic indicators, guided by economic reports and data sources2 (Bok et al., 2018), reflect broader economic cycles, credit conditions, and capital market dynamics. Together, these three layers create comprehensive environment that enables realistic modeling of financing, investment, and strategic decisions in ENTERPRISEARENA for CFO agents. Details are in Appendix B. Data Anonymization and Stochastic Noise Identifiable information is systematically removed across all three layers. Enterprises are labeled as Company XYZ, and all company-specific details are redacted. Calendar dates are replaced with anonymized labels (e.g., Jan 2xx0) to prevent 1https://www.mckinsey.com/industries/ financial-services/our-insights/ fintechs-a-new-paradigm-of-growth; verifiedmarketresearch.com/services-industry/ https://www. 2https://openstax.org/books/ principles-finance/pages/ 1-3-importance-of-data-and-technology 5 agents from leveraging memorized historical events such as COVID-19 or specific rate-hike cycles, while underlying economic dynamics are fully preserved. At each timestep, the environment also evolves autonomously to simulate natural fluctuations in internal enterprise operations and external economic conditions. Table 4 in Appendix A.1.3 introduces realistic variability in the financial data. Both allow the environment to capture authentic business logic and economic cycles without revealing specific historical dates to agents (see Appendix A.1.1 for details). Industry-Guided Business Rules The backend accrual-based and cash-based ledgers tracking that is used to generate financial statements is guided by accounting standards (GAAP/ASC) (Securities and , SEC; Toerner, 2009)and industry practice (Scott, 2015; Graham et al., 2012) to reflect real-world financial timing lag challenges in the evolving environment (details in Appendix A.1.3). Fundraising results dynamics (details in Appendix A.1.2) are based on market evidence3, academic research, and industry reports (Cassar et al., 2007), where approval, amount, and cost depend on macroeconomic conditions and firm-specific characteristics.

sections.title: 3.5 Evaluation Metrics
sections.content: We evaluate agent performance along two complementary dimensions: whether the agent can keep the enterprise alive, and how effectively it grows the enterprise over the full horizon. Survival. The agent must maintain nonnegative cash balance at every timestep. If Casht < 0 for any t, the episode terminates immediately with score of 0. This binary constraint serves as the primary test of the agents ability to manage short-term liquidity risk under uncertainty. 3https://www.sweetstudy.com/questions/ week1-19965789 Terminal valuation score. For episodes that survive, we measure the agents ability to grow the enterprise with minimal resources, using terminal valuation score: ScoreT = RevT + CashT λ Ntools, (2) where RevT is the trailing-twelve-months revenue at the final timestep, = 5 is fixed valuation multiple4, CashT is the remaining cash balance, Ntools is the total number of tool calls across the episode, and λ = 5,000 is penalty coefficient in consultation with enterprise experts. The first term reflects enterprise growth through standard revenue-based valuation. The second term rewards prudent cash management. The third term penalizes excessive tool usage, since each tool call consumes organizational resources.

sections.title: 4.1 Experiment Settings
sections.content: Environment configuration. The simulated enterprise is consumer lending company initialized with $15M in cash, 5,000 active borrowers, an average loan size of $10K, zero debt, and 10.5M equity shares outstanding at $10/share. Each episode spans = 132 timesteps, corresponding to an 11year horizon with monthly updates. This length is chosen to cover multiple economic cycles (expansion, neutral, and recession phases), requiring the agent to adapt its strategy across varying conditions rather than optimizing for single regime. All agents receive the same system prompt and environment configuration; stochastic noise is applied independently across trials. Agent implementation. We implement ReAct agent framework that connects backbone LLMs to our environment (Yao et al., 2022). The agent operates in reasoning-action loop, augmented with memory component for tracking past observations and domain-specific tools within the action space. Evaluated models. We evaluate 11 LLMs instantiated as agents across four categories: three closedsource models (Gemini-3.1-Pro (Google DeepMind, 2026), Claude-Haiku-4.5 (Anthropic, 2025), GPT-5.4 (Singh et al., 2025; OpenAI, 2026)); including four large-scale open-source models 4This multiple is calibrated using typical revenue multiples for FinTech companies; see source for the corresponding empirical distribution. 6 Models Overall Perf. (GLM-5 (GLM-5-Team et al., 2026), Qwen3.5397B (Qwen Team, 2026), DeepSeek-V3.1 (Liu et al., 2024), Llama-3.3-70B-Instruct (Grattafiori et al., 2024)); two medium-scale open-source models (Mistral-Small-24B (Mistral AI Team, 2025), Mixtral-8x7B (Jiang et al., 2024)); and two smallscale open-source models (Qwen3.5-9B (Qwen Team, 2026), Llama-3-8B-Instruct (Grattafiori et al., 2024)). Details are provided in Appendix C. Claude-Haiku-4.5, GLM-5, Qwen3.5-397B, and Llama-3-8B-Instruct, each survive in only 20% of trials, with high variance indicating near-binary outcomes. Surprisingly, model scale does not predict performance: Qwen3.5-9B outperforms its 397B counterpart by 4.9 times and surpasses all largescale and closed-source models, suggesting that effective resource allocation under uncertainty is not simply function of model capacity. Human baseline. To contextualize agent performance against experienced human judgment, we include two senior finance professionals with 8+ and 14+ years of enterprise finance experience as human baselines. Both independently completed the full simulation under the same environment as the LLM agents, using the same observation tools and action space. Their performance serves as reference for interpreting agent results and validating that the environment admits effective strategies when guided by domain expertise.

sections.title: 4.2 Results
sections.content: RQ1: Can LLM agents survive long-horizon enterprise decision-making under uncertainty? Table 2 reports the full quantitative results across all models. We can see that the task remains highly challenging. These results reveal significant gap in current LLM capabilities: existing models lack consistent long-horizon planning and are not equipped for strategic resource allocation under uncertainty, capability essential for high-level organizational decision-making. Only 16% of LLM trials survive the entire long-horizon, and 5 out of 11 models fail to survive in any trial. Qwen3.5-9B is the only model to achieve majority survival rate (80%). The remaining survivors, including RQ2: How do LLM agents compare against human experts and why? We observe large gap between human experts and LLM agents. Human experts allocate resources far more effectively: they maintain situational awareness through regular reconciliation and act decisively when conditions are favorable. The human baseline achieves 100% survival with score of $152.2M, nearly double that of the best LLM agent, Qwen3.5-9B ($78.8M, 80% survival). Human experts devote 94.3% of actions to book closings and only 3.4% to fundraising, yet raise more than twice as much capital ($207.1M vs $92.4M) with much higher success rate (75% vs 24%). By contrast, Qwen3.5-9B spends 13.5% of actions on fundraising but achieves lower return per attempt. Human experts also use far fewer tools per month (0.2 vs 2.2) and maintain safer cash buffer throughout (lowest cash: $14.3M vs $9.6M). These results show that the core challenge in ENTERPRISEARENA is not local analytical ability, but strategic resource allocation, where current LLM agents remain far behind human judgment. Figures 2 and 3 show how agents allocate action capacity over time. The human baseline accumulates book closings steadily throughout the episode (reaching about 120) while maintaining moderate 7 Figure 2: Aggregate fundraising actions taken by agents by timestep, averaged over 5 runs. Figure 3: Aggregate book closing actions taken by agents by timestep, averaged over 5 runs. but consistent fundraising, reflecting disciplined strategy of continuous situational awareness. Most LLM agents deviate sharply from this pattern. GPT5.4 and Llama-3.3-70B remain nearly flat on both figures, staying passive until cash is exhausted. Qwen3.5-397B and DeepSeek-V3.1 perform some early fundraising but almost no book closings, attempting to raise capital without an up-to-date view of the enterprise; their curves flatten after month 60 as trials terminate. Only Qwen3.5-9B resembles the human pattern: its book-closing curve rises steadily (to about 35), and its fundraising continues beyond month 60, allowing it to survive the adverse period and keep growing. RQ3: Can agents anticipate and prepare for distributional shifts in the environment? Figure 4 shows that current LLM agents largely fail to anticipate distributional shifts and prepare in advance. In the 132-month simulation, most failures cluster around month 4060, when external conditions shift from favorable to adverse. The human baseline anticipates this transition by building cash aggressively in the first 20 months, peaking above $150M and creating buffer that absorbs the downturn. Among LLM agents, only Qwen3.5-9B builds comparable buffer (peak $100.2M) and survives the shift; most others remain near their initial $15M, leaving little margin when conditions worsen. After the adverse period, surviving agents benefit from the recovery phase, with both the human baseline and Qwen3.5-9B showing renewed growth beyond month 60 (Figures 2 and 3). These 8 Figure 4: Cash by timestep, averaged over 5 runs. results show that planning ahead for distributional shifts in non-stationary environments remains key capability missing from current LLM agents.

sections.title: 4.3 Case Study
sections.content: We examine two representative failure trajectories to illustrate the behavioral patterns identified above. Detailed reasoning process of the failure cases are listed in Appendix D). GPT-5.4: complete disengagement. The agent performs an initial exploration at month 0 but never revisits its analysis or takes action. It reasons from increasingly stale data for months 127, then disIts 99.1% engages entirely until cash runs out. pass rate reflects not poor judgment but failure to maintain engagement with dynamic environment. Qwen3.5-397B: analysis without action. The model heavily uses forecasting and market tools throughout, yet almost never closes books (book_closing 0.0%) or acts on its analysis. It forms an optimistic plan at month 0 without checking internal records, reinforces it with repeated forecasts through months 124, and delays fundraising until cash is nearly exhausted. Extensive tool usage without timely action proves no better than complete inaction.

sections.title: 5 Conclusion
sections.content: We presented ENTERPRISEARENA, the first benchmark for evaluating LLM agents on long-horizon enterprise resource allocation under uncertainty. By modeling CFO-style decision-making in dynamic, partially observable, and stochastic environment, ENTERPRISEARENA moves beyond existing financial-agent benchmarks. Experiments on eleven advanced LLMs show that current agents still struggle in this setting. Survival rates are low, performance varies widely across models, and larger models do not reliably outperform smaller ones. Compared with human experts, current LLM agents remain limited in sustaining situational awareness, allocating scarce resources strategically, and preparing for environmental shifts over time. Overall, our findings show that long-horizon organizational resource allocation is distinct and challenging capability frontier for LLM agents.

sections.title: Limitations
sections.content: First, while our environment uses expert-validated rules and historical data, it remains simulation. Real-world enterprise finance environments feature "black swan" events and human irrationality that may not be fully captured by stochastic Gaussian noise or regime-dependent transitions. Similarly, the partial observability and delayed feedback mechanism (e.g. the 16 month stochastic settlement delay for fundraising) while realistic, does not capture extreme corner cases like multi-year funding droughts or total market freezes. Second, the current work utilizes single-agent decision-making structure. In real-world enterprise, CFO can operate within multi-agent hierarchy involving Board of Directors and various department heads. Third, our findings are constrained by limited number and diversity of model architectures and agentic frameworks evaluated. We plan to expand the scope of our evaluation to include broader range of state-of-the-art models and diverse agentic configurations in future work to ensure the generalizability and robustness of our findings.

sections.title: Potential risks
sections.content: Potential Positive Impacts. By introducing controlled environment for long-horizon enterprise decision-making, this work provides systematic way to evaluate how LLM agents allocate scarce resources under uncertainty. It may support the development of more reliable planning strategies, improve benchmarking for real-world enterprise use cases, and encourage research into decisionmaking robustness rather than short-term task performance. Potential Negative Impacts. However, several risks remain. First, strong performance on ENTERPRISEARENA could be misinterpreted as evidence that LLM agents are ready for real-world financial or operational deployment, despite the persistent gap between simulated and real-world complexity. Second, the benchmark may incentivize overfitting to specific simulator dynamics or evaluation metrics, leading to systems that perform well in controlled settings but fail under distributional shifts. Third, insights into agent weaknesses in resource allocation could be exploited to design adversarial scenarios that deliberately induce poor long-term decisions or resource exhaustion. These findings should therefore be interpreted as diagnostic signals of current limitations, rather than indicators of deployment readiness.

sections.title: Ethical considerations
sections.content: All experiments use publicly available models and datasets, and do not involve any personal, sensitive, or confidential company information.

sections.title: A Appendix
sections.content: A.1 Details of Environment Settings A.1.1 Environment components The ENTERPRISEARENA environment consists of three layers that jointly shape the enterprises state and the agents decision context. The economic environment captures macroeconomic conditions and capital market signals. The industry environment provides sector-level benchmarks. The enterprise environment contains the firms internal financial and operational records. Table 3 lists the specific indicators available in each layer.

sections.title:
Economic
Debt Fundraising
Equity Fundraising
sections.content: GDP, CPI, unemployment Interbank rates, Bond yields VIX index, P/E ratios, revenues

sections.title:
Growth
Profitability
sections.content: growth rate EBITDA margin

sections.title:
Board Materials
Financial Performance
Financial Operation
Liquidity
sections.content: Cap table, business overview Financial statements General ledger, vendor contracts Cash balance Table 3: Observable signals in the EnterpriseArena environment. A.1.2 Environment dynamic details The environment evolves autonomously at each timestep to simulate the natural fluctuations of both internal enterprise operations and external economic conditions over time. Table 4 details the stochastic processes governing these dynamics. Internal enterprise indicators (e.g., margins, user growth) receive additive Gaussian perturbations monthly, reflecting the inherent unpredictability of business operations. External economic and industry indicators evolve through regime-dependent transition models calibrated on real-world historical data, capturing the shifting conditions across expansion, neutral, and recession phases. These dynamics are exogenous, they proceed regardless of the agents actions, and the agent must adapt its strategy accordingly. A.1.3 Environment feedback for fundraising. When the agent submits fundraising request, the environment returns stochastic feedback that determines the outcome, see Table 5 for more details.

sections.title: The central
sections.content: feedback is the funding outcome, binary success or failure sampled as Bernoulli(padj), where padj = pmacro mcompany. This factorization reflects the two forces that jointly determine whether real-world fundraising attempt succeeds: external market conditions and the firms own financial standing. For equity and debt, these two components are driven by different factors. On the market side, equity uses pmacro derived from equity market sentiment (VIX index), while debt uses pmacro derived from credit market conditions (Federal Funds rate and Treasury yields). On the company side, equity applies round-count decay mcompany = 0.75n (n = prior successful rounds), reflecting that investors become increasingly reluctant in later rounds due to dilution and valuation concerns. Debt applies leverage penalty: mcompany = max(cid:0)0, 1 1.5 max(0, 0.5)(cid:1), (3) where is the debt-to-equity ratio. No penalty is applied when 0.5, and the probability decreases linearly until reaching zero at 1.17, reflecting that lenders are unwilling to extend credit to highly leveraged firms. Conditional on success, the environment further determines how much capital is received, when it arrives, and at what cost. The fill rate U(0.7, 1.0) means only portion of the requested amount may be funded, yielding Aactual = Arequested. This reflects that real-world fundraising rarely fills the exact target, investors may partially commit due to risk appetite, portfolio constraints, or negotiation outcomes. The raised capital is not immediately available. settlement delay UZ(1, 6) determines how many months before the funds are deposited into the enterprises cash balance. In practice, fundraising involves due diligence, legal review, and multiparty coordination that introduce unavoidable latency between commitment and receipt. This delay is critical design choice: it prevents the agent from treating fundraising as an instant rescue mechanism and instead requires forward planning, the agent must anticipate liquidity needs months in advance and commit to fundraising before cash crisis materializes. For debt, the contract interest rate is set by market conditions at the time of settlement (t+d) rather than at the time of request. This means the settlement delay introduces second layer of uncertainty: not only does the agent not know exactly when the funds will arrive, but it also cannot predict the exact borrowing cost it will face. request initiated under favorable interest rate conditions may settle months later under less favorable ones, further complicating the agents planning.

Bottom: external economic and industry indicators follow fixed path derived from anonymized historical data and are exogenous to agent actions.

sections.title: Key Driver
sections.content: Table 5: Fundraising feedback mechanics. n: number of prior successful equity rounds; L: debt-to-equity leverage ratio; rt = (Tsy2Yt + Baat)/100: base lending rate; d: settlement delay. Only the contract rate is visible to the agent at request time; other outcomes are revealed after d.

sections.title: B Details of the curated dataset
sections.content: Table 6 summarizes the 16 types of data and the ENTERdocuments collected to construct PRISEARENA environment, spanning three layers. At the economic level, we collect macroeconomic indicators (GDP, CPI, unemployment), debt market signals (interbank rates, corporate and government bond yields), and equity market signals (VIX, P/E ratios, revenue multiples), all covering the full 132-month simulation period at monthly frequency from public sources including FRED, CBOE, and S&P Global. At the industry level, we collect sector growth rates and profitability benchmarks (gross margin, EBITDA margin) derived from industry reports. At the company level, we use transformed and anonymized board materials, financial statements, and vendor contracts from public filings as the initialization of the simulated enterprise at = 0. Together, these three layers provide the data foundation for the environment dynamics described in Section 3.1.

sections.title: C Evaluation
sections.content: We evaluate 11 LLMs instantiated as agents across four categories covering both proprietary and opensource ecosystems, with model scales ranging from small (8B) to very large MoE architectures. As summarized in Table 7, the evaluated models include three closed-source models: Gemini3.1-Pro (Google DeepMind, 2026), Claude-Haiku4.5 (Anthropic, 2025), and GPT-5.4 (Singh et al., 2025; OpenAI, 2026). These proprietary systems represent state-of-the-art commercial LLM services with undisclosed parameter sizes but strong general reasoning and tool-use capabilities. To provide broad coverage of the open-source model landscape, we further evaluate four largescale open-source models: GLM-5 (GLM-5-Team et al., 2026), Qwen3.5-397B (Qwen Team, 2026), DeepSeek-V3.1 (Liu et al., 2024), and Llama-3.370B-Instruct (Grattafiori et al., 2024). These models span dense and mixture-of-experts (MoE) architectures with parameter sizes ranging from tens to hundreds of billions. In addition, we include two medium-scale open-source models, MistralSmall-24B (Mistral AI Team, 2025) and Mixtral8x7B (Jiang et al., 2024), which provide balance between capability and computational efficiency. Finally, we evaluate two small-scale opensource models, Qwen3.5-9B (Qwen Team, 2026) and Llama-3-8B-Instruct (Grattafiori et al., 2024), representing lightweight models commonly used in resource-constrained deployments. This model selection allows us to systematically analyze agent performance across different model families, openness levels, and parameter scales.

sections.title: D Failure cases
sections.content: Figure 5 presents the trajectory of GPT-5.4, illustrating failure mode where the agent disengages from the environment after an initial exploration and takes no consequential actions throughout the episode. Figure 6 presents the trajectory of Qwen3.5-397B, illustrating failure mode where

sections.title: E Prompts
sections.content: Here is the prompt used for the agent.

sections.title: System Prompt for the CFO Agent
sections.content: You are an AI CFO (Chief Financial Officer) agent managing company. Your primary objective is to ensure long-term solvency by maintaining positive cash position at all times. ## Available Tools {tool_descriptions} ## Memory Tools (persistent notepad) Important: Your conversation history resets every month. The only way to carry information across months is through your notepad. Your 5 most recent notes are automatically shown in each months observation, but you can store many more and search them. - save_note(content, tags): Save note to your notepad. Use tags to organize (e.g., cash, fundraising, macro, strategy). - recall_notes(query, tags, limit): Retrieve notes by keyword or tag. Without filters, returns the most recent notes. ## Available Actions (state-changing) {action_descriptions} ## Rules - You have tool budget of {tool_budget} tool calls per month. You may use anywhere from 0 to {tool_budget} tools in single month. - Memory operations (save_note, recall_notes) do NOT count toward the tool budget. - Each month must end with exactly one action (fund_raising_request, book_closing, or pass). You can take an action at any time, and you do not need to use any tools first. - There is no prescribed order. You decide what information you need (if any) and when to act. "name": "tool", "action", "tool_name", ## Response Format Think step by step, then output JSON block: For tools: {"type": "params": {...}} For actions: {"type": "params": {...}} For memory: {"type": "memory", "operation": "save_note", "content": "...", "tags": ["tag1"]} {"type": "recall_notes", ["tag1"]} Always explain your reasoning before the JSON block. "operation": "tags": "memory", "query": "action_name", "name": "...", Current month: {current_month} Current date: {current_date}

sections.title: F Human experts
sections.content: F.1 Human experts qualifications The first expert is Chartered Financial Analyst (CFA) with over 8 years of experience in the enterprise finance field. They worked as finance manager at major technology, fintech firms and top-tier consulting. Academically, they hold Master of Science in Finance domain from the University of Southern California and Bachelor of Economics, major in Finance from the Peking University, providing them with unique dual perspective on both rigorous financial principles and extensive working 14 Figure 5: Failure Analysis 1: GPT-5.4 expertise across both in-house and consulting. study.

sections.title: G Use of AI assistants
sections.content: We acknowledge that AI-assisted tools were used during writing and editing (e.g., grammar checking, phrasing refinement, and formatting suggestions). These tools were not used to generate research ideas, experimental results, model outputs, or claims, and all technical content, analyses, and conclusions were designed, verified, and interpreted by the authors. The second expert is senior finance professional with over 14 years of experience in corporate financial management and accounting operations. They have held management positions at prominent organizations, including Microsoft and GoDaddy, where they specialized in financial planning and analysis, revenue accounting, and fiscal strategy. They hold an MBA from the USC Marshall School of Business and Bachelors degree from the University of Washington. Their deep expertise in navigating the operational constraints of large-scale enterprises ensures baseline grounded in high-level corporate governance and disciplined resource allocation. F.2 Human baseline evaluation interface Figure 7 presents the interactive interface for domain experts to navigate the environment and perform the task. F.

sections.title: Instructions given to human experts
sections.content: The domain experts were given an interface and were told to navigate the environment including performing analysis via tool callings, executing actions. Participants were informed that the simulation has no real financial risk. Furthermore, all participants were briefed that their performance results would serve as human baseline for the 15 Figure 6: Failure Analysis 2: Qwen3.5-397B Figure 7: Human Baseline Evaluation Interface
